[[File:Thetranquildisruption.png|590px|link=]]













Level 921, also known as “Tranquil Disruption,” is a vast enclosed space visually dominated by white and pink tones. Its overall structure forms an extremely complex and nearly infinitely extending vertical architectural system. However, due to the highly disordered light received by cells, individuals within the area are unable to directly perceive this complexity while present inside it. The most space-occupying object is an endless spiral staircase, which extends both upward and downward along a non-physical central axis. When stepped on, the temperature of the entire staircase becomes −2°C, but it returns to its original state once no longer touched. The staircase possesses irregular segmented sections; each segment has its own style and design, often appearing extremely unnatural. The walls serve as the outer boundary of the spiral staircase. They are not necessarily connected to the staircase itself, but they usually remain within three meters of it. The walls occasionally throw out certain items, though these are always impractical device. On rare occasions, the walls may form narrow, elongated transparent openings with no barrier to the outside. Beyond them lies a cloudless sky of pure blue with no visible celestial bodies. Tests have shown that it is impossible to observe the enclosed structure or its internal contents from the outside. All things in this level are made of a special iridescent liquid with no clear boundaries between its colors. Its texture resembles liquid gallium, but it possesses neither a vaporization point nor a freezing point. Besides functioning as a construction material, it can also exist while floating in midair. It is especially abundant in the central hollow of the staircase, where it may even fill the space entirely. Free-floating liquid appears slightly white and cannot be stepped on, which is the primary method for distinguishing between the two forms. Gravity behaves abnormally. If the staircase sections have no railings or structures preventing falls, and wanderers may experience changes in their local plane of orientation. This occurs because the angle under their feet (half of the angle between the forward-tilting original ground and another surface) causes their perceived surface of standing to shift. If a wanderer rotates exactly 180 degrees, they will not only observe the expected inverted surroundings but will also manifest severe vomiting. All non-living objects remain unaffected. Attempts to take photographs are ineffective. Images appear with extremely oversaturated distortion. Within a few seconds, the photographed scene becomes reality: the liquid material rapidly moves from elsewhere to the photographed location, recreating the captured state. Sound is similarly distorted. Any signals or speech transmitted from this location become difficult to understand, and attempting to record and replay audio will intensify the distortion into static noise. From an objective perspective, this level exists in a vacuum with no passage of time (the fact that audio retains a measurable duration is itself considered anomalous). The absence of time flow negates the suffocation that the vacuum would normally cause. However, this does not mean wanderers are free from harm; injuries can still occur, though they must be psychological or self-inflicted. Notably, the effects of Almond Water become ineffective here due to the lack of time flow and should not be relied upon to alleviate mental distress.
Entities
This level is classified as a mental and environmental hazard level, and no entities have currently been discovered within it.
Entrances
Entrances appear in levels where at least four numbers occur consecutively, and the method of entry between them follows an ascending order from small to large. Near the entrance leading to this level, anomalies that should not occur in that level will appear. Wanderers familiar with their surroundings may notice these irregularities.
Exits
'''1.''' If one remains in this level for a sufficiently long time, it is possible to transition to [[Level 54]] or [[Level 922]], though this likely requires severe visual collapse, extreme psychological instability, or total color blindness. '''2.''' Descending 9223372036854775807 steps will lead to [[Level 398]]. Ascending leads to an unknown destination (speculated to be [[Level 1000]]). Due to the nature of this level, these exits are not considered feasible. As the wanderer falls through this twenty-three to twenty-six year abyss, they will inevitably attempt to end their life or halt the persistent descent; climbing upward, remains an impossibility.

















































































"Documentation is so boring, I want to hear a story."
"This is to protect yourself."
"It’s not like anything's going to happen..."
"No means no."
"I don’t care, I don’t care!"
"Why are you so persistent?"
"I hate being bored."
"Fine... I do have a story I can tell."
Colorful Liquid Spiral Staircase
A person walks across a chrome-plated parking lot, accompanied by his only companion. The ground stretches across his field of vision like a luminous surface without end, and the white light is sharp and hard, like the edge of metal ground to a blade, refracting again and again at the edges of sight. Thick industrial goggles compress the world into a dim gray filter, yet even so, the brilliance still seeps in through every seam, piercing deep into the eyes. Holding the thing in his hand, a piece of junk with no merit other than providing energy, the man’s gaze drifts absentmindedly. He had never imagined that he would fall so low as to eat metal. A damned idea arises naturally in his mind: after leaving this place, he could wrap it in chocolate packaging, pretend it’s some delicious dessert, and sell it. But for now, he can only grit his teeth and swallow it. A flavor that no food should possess floods his taste buds, nauseating enough to make him want to vomit. Prolonged exposure begins to produce certain visual distortions. The pillars that were once straight gradually twist in the distance, becoming misaligned, colliding with impossible, erroneous boundaries. Looking down at the mirror-like ground beneath his feet, he sees his own distorted, pallid face reflected back. Observing all this only makes him want to destroy this place as soon as possible, but he has no such ability, because he isn’t some superpowered being. He’s just an ordinary person. The parking spaces might be gradually decreasing, but the intense reflections make it impossible for him to be sure. If there were cars, things would be much easier, he could traverse this place quickly. Not having a driver’s license had once been the greatest regret of his life, a symbol of failed adulthood. Logically, this place should grant him some kind of transcendental compensation or turning point. But of course, in a place with so little logic, that counts for nothing. Nothing here carries a complete meaning. His companion remains by his side. The presence makes no sound and performs no special action, merely maintaining a steady pace, moving forward together with him. Looking at that calm, untroubled profile, the long-suppressed resentment deep within him begins to stir. He cannot understand how this person can remain completely free of unease. Two moving reflections continue onward; aside from them, everything around seems utterly dead, nothing else remaining. He has already decided to treat an empty room as his new place of residence. This endless place truly has no meaning, and no exit can be found. At the very least, that spot might serve as a stable area where he can accept the current reality. He knows his mind is already becoming somewhat unclear, but there is no better method. Things produced in this century are certainly more useful than the pull-tabs from centuries ago, the former would merely send him back to the era of Nokia phones. The thing he had pinned such hopes on seems, at the very edge of his vision, to appear faintly and precariously. The wall is still made of the same material, yet as a whole it feels like something he has not seen for a long time. He decides to wait a few more minutes before confirming it. His heart tells him to leave these place, but the urge cannot be dispelled; he can only resist it by wasting time. The reflections on the wall refuse to cooperate, continuing their troublesome antics again and again. Hands of the clock spin wildly in the invisible; time stubbornly maintains its march forward, meanly leaving behind no trace to prove it has ever passed. Under the white glare, the void grows exponentially. The only thing left that confirms the existence of this abstract concept is the dull echo of footsteps striking the ground. In solitude the sound is amplified, so much so that it feels like a volcanic eruption, deafening. This place can never become familiar. It always feels strange and chilling, frightening in a way that never fades. Each corner looks exactly the same as the last, refusing to provide any clear coordinates. His original sense of direction collapses under repeated futility and extreme physical exhaustion. He can only struggle onward, pressing the W key by random chance. Desolate and bizarre, pitiful yet gleaming. He has forgotten how he came here. How he wishes it were merely an accident, but he knows it was planned, or at least improvised. He also knows that the cheapest form of self-consolation in this world is to blame disasters on misfortune. Disasters created by one’s own hands bring pain and blindness; by now everything is already settled, and there is no room left to turn things around. Once again he looks at the one beside him—calm, cruel, indifferent to suffering. Perhaps he himself believes that ordinary people must forever live beneath fear, and for the sake of fairness, everyone should obey that rule. He hates that indifference, hates the state that might even be called mockery. No one should receive redemption. Let fear be born anew if it must; anything was better than this abyss of emotionless void. The hourglass has run dry; the time his neurons have left for hesitation is officially over. He readjusts his goggles and prepares to walk toward the shadow he glimpsed earlier, the one that might be room. If he delays any longer, he might lose it again. If that place can truly let him stop for a while, grant him a brief rest, it would already be a fortunate thing. No matter how others see it, achieving even this much in the midst of despair would not be so bad. But actually reaching it would require almost a miracle. Lighting, structural order—everywhere is a shit challenge. He exerts his greatest effort to search for that tiny place. Who knows how much time passes, yet he still finds nothing. Only after several hours does it flash into view once more. The time the journey required exceeded expectations by more than tenfold—truly embarrassing. Slow, so slow... or he just merely trapped in an endless loop. The door was the same nauseating cold chrome. When he pushed it open, the sharp grinding sound seemed to mock his impoverished survival choices. He remembered cartoons and films he had seen before, people surviving on deserted islands at least had coconuts or something to confide in. All he had was endless transition metals and a companion who gave no response whatsoever. Even after finally seeing the thing itself, he felt no relief. That vile sensation still hung over him. He looked inside: empty—unless things that could not be taken by any means counted. Between pure energy and tangible matter there remained a mutual repulsion. The two could never achieve final fusion or unity within this dimension, and that had caused all kinds of problems for him. Walls within sight, though not yet touched, seemed already to exude a bone-chilling cold that seeped into the marrow. He longed to collapse on the spot, but until he personally confirmed that reality matched his suspicion, he could not allow himself to do so. Holding his breath, fingertips trembling, he reached forward. In that fragile touch, as if walking on thin ice, he humbly prayed that the sensation would align with his shattered hope. Burning pain surged like a tide. He stared at the hand now trembling slightly; the skin was rapidly reddening and swelling before his eyes, destined to become clusters of clear yet painful blisters. So indeed there was nothing here that could be used to determine anything. He let out a bitter smile, deeply disappointed—because there was nothing at all that could be made to work. He set down his backpack. The black fabric didn’t quite look black. He searched inside for the first-aid kit. The bag was stuffed full of things, strangely opposite to the emptiness of where he stood, but that hardly mattered. In the process he realized he hadn’t brought any tools at all, though even if he had, they would probably have been useless. The feeling of being stuck pressed across his arm like a nightmare. Suddenly he found the first-aid kit. He pulled hard and finally managed to drag it out. Things scattered everywhere. His gaze swept anxiously through the chaos; instinctively he grabbed the nearest cold, hard object and, with extreme frustration, slammed it down to vent his anger. But the direction shifted, it became a throw, carving a sharp and dangerous arc through the air. A slicing sound reached his cochlea. And who was the victim? There was no one else. Slowly he raised his head, and was startled. His companion looked at him with a puzzled expression. For the first time in this place, that person showed a facial expression. But that wasn’t the main point. The main point was the wound on the companion’s leg—clearly colorless. There was no hemoglobin. This should not have happened. There was nothing like this here. Such a phenomenon was absolutely impossible in this place. He tried to explain the situation to himself, to give himself a reasonable interpretation, perhaps it was an illusion caused by the white light. But even if the light was overwhelming, it could not erase color to such an absurd degree. The bright iron-red of blood could not be concealed. That was common sense. He shoved away the companion who had once walked beside him, trying to push away some heavy fate itself, like a madman, he sprinted toward the exit. His chest pounded. He didn’t know whether that thing was following him or not. His terror was worse than ever before. He fled from something that might lead to its death, running toward an unknown destination. NEVERNESS THREE

Perhaps thousands of steps, perhaps tens of thousands. He could not clearly understand how many remained, because he was going insane. His legs had long since lost all sensation, and only a trace of rational force moved his feet forward. He lifted his head. Above him was still the endless spiral submerged in white light; below was a vivid crimson darkness formed from red light, proving his own helplessness.

He struck the steps with force as a test, because they were not solid. Forcing the crack open and staring into it, the expected result did not appear. Instead, there was a field of pure white void. That whiteness ignored the laws of physics as it gazed at him, and he allowed his soul to sink into that endless stare. The pure white surged in like a tide, washing away every heavy memory and upset of his past until nothing remained.

{{Collapse|num=1|initial=
He slowly closed his eyes, letting his eyelids shut out the noise of reality. In the depths of his consciousness, he began to sketch a quiet illusion. He dreamed of lying down, dreamed of lying there comfortably. It was painfully obvious, he had abandoned life. But here, nothing would die because of anything, and there were no explanations left anymore.
|final=|body=

But here, nothing would die because of anything, and there were no explanations left anymore...?
